title: Attack rhythm
principles: Attack rhythm describes how damage instances are initiated and repeated; it must not be treated as a damage-delivery type.
Automatic and continuous triggers can support repeated pressure, but practical application still depends on contact, magazine duration, reload downtime, and ammunition.
Semi-automatic behavior depends on repeated manual inputs and should not be described as continuous merely because the listed fire rate is high.
Burst behavior must preserve burst count and intra-burst delay when supplied; listed fire rate alone may not represent the complete burst cycle.
Charge behavior trades immediate release for a delayed attack and must be evaluated from supplied charge evidence rather than inferred from damage magnitude.
Active or remotely triggered behavior separates deployment from detonation and should not be described as ordinary semi-automatic fire.
Duplex behavior may create separate press and release events; do not collapse it into conventional burst behavior unless the supplied attack modes support that interpretation.
A weapon may contain several attack rhythms across its modes; conclusions must preserve mode boundaries.

automatic: The weapon supports repeated fire while the input is maintained.
Sustained pressure remains conditional on magazine, reload, ammunition, and target contact.
semi_automatic: Each attack requires a separate input.
Listed fire rate represents potential cadence rather than guaranteed practical cadence.
burst: Each activation releases a supplied sequence of attacks.
Burst count and delay determine how concentrated the delivery is.
charge: The attack includes an explicit charge stage before release.
Charge time is an operational cost only when the corresponding field is supplied.
continuous: Damage is applied through maintained activation.
Continuous activation does not establish internal tick frequency.
active: The weapon separates placement or firing from a later activation event.
Its rhythm should be described as staged rather than conventional repeated fire.
duplex: The trigger can create distinct press and release behavior.
The two events must not be assumed to share identical statistics.
undetermined: The supplied trigger and mode data are insufficient to classify attack rhythm confidently.

exceptions: Do not infer practical click speed from the listed semi-automatic fire rate.
Do not infer charge time from a charge label when no timing value is supplied.
Do not treat continuous delivery as uninterrupted mission-long firing.
Do not merge different attack modes into one rhythm.
